<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- All files are pure data (JSON/JSONL/Markdown) with no imports, producing correct isolation — no application code should live here
- The presence of both execution-log.jsonl and execution-log.1.jsonl suggests log rotation is active; ensure the rotation strategy is documented to avoid unbounded log growth
- workflow.md alongside machine-readable JSON provides a human-readable audit trail, which is a useful pattern for debugging agent runs
- Zero coupling is expected and correct — these are data files, not modules. Any import edges into this zone would indicate a structural problem.
- Two execution log files (execution-log.jsonl, execution-log.1.jsonl) indicate log rotation; confirm the rotation limit is configured to prevent unbounded disk growth in long-running projects.
- pending-proposals.json and acknowledged-findings.json form a lightweight review workflow; this pattern cleanly separates proposed changes from accepted state in prd.json.
- rex-runtime-state is the only zone in the entire graph that is both a leaf (no outgoing edges) and a root (no incoming edges) — it is a true island, which is correct for a data-files-only zone and confirms no application code has accidentally imported from it
- rex-runtime-state is a fully isolated island (no import edges in either direction) — any future edge appearing here should be treated as a structural regression and blocked in CI.
- 'rex-runtime-state' is the only zone named after runtime state rather than a functional or package role — all other zones use functional names (hench-agent-engine, architecture-documentation) or package names (web-package-shell). This naming inconsistency makes the zone harder to locate by role.
- Rename zone 'rex-runtime-state' to 'rex-persistence' or 'rex-data-store' to match the functional naming convention used by every other zone. The current name describes deployment state rather than architectural role, making it inconsistent with zone discovery patterns.

</insights>
